era_context: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor is consolidating power and launching ambitious architectural and maritime projects. In Europe, the Council of Constance is concluding its sessions, having condemned Jan Hus and other reformers. Religious tensions in Bohemia are escalating toward open conflict. The Italian Renaissance is emerging. The Ottoman Empire continues expansion in the Balkans. Portuguese maritime exploration is beginning.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indigenous Americas (no written records in European tradition)
Central Asian steppes and Inner Asia (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; religious reform movement condemned
events.description: The Council of Constance, which had been in session since 1414, concludes its work in 1418. The council had condemned Jan Hus to death in 1415 and had also condemned the teachings of John Wycliffe and other reformers. The council's decisions regarding religious authority, church reform, and the condemnation of heresy remain controversial, particularly in Bohemia, where Hus's followers continue to resist papal authority. The council's failure to address the underlying grievances that motivated reform movements contributes to the escalation of religious conflict in Central Europe, particularly the Hussite Wars which will begin in 1419.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, council participant)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees
Historiographic consensus on the Council of Constance

events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance concluded in 1418. The date is well-established in contemporary records and modern scholarship.
cross_references: 1415-001 (Jan Hus execution at Council of Constance)

events.title: Zheng He's fourth treasure voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's fourth major maritime expedition returns to China after visiting East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and ports throughout the Indian Ocean. The voyage continues the pattern of massive Ming maritime operations, with fleets of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel. These expeditions serve diplomatic, commercial, and intelligence-gathering purposes, establishing Ming tributary relationships and prestige across the Indian Ocean world. The voyages represent the apex of early 15th-century maritime technology and demonstrate Chinese naval superiority.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Luo Maodeng, The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingya Shenglan)

events.certainty_note: The return of Zheng He's fourth voyage in 1418 is documented in Ming imperial records. The voyage sequence and dates are well-established.

events.title: Forbidden City construction continues in Beijing
events.description: Construction of the Forbidden City (紫禁城), the imperial palace complex in Beijing, continues under the Yongle Emperor's direction. Work began in 1406 and will be largely finished by 1420. By 1418, the major structural elements are in place, and work is proceeding on interior decoration, furnishings, and landscaping. The complex represents an extraordinary feat of architectural and logistical coordination, employing hundreds of thousands of workers and incorporating advanced timber-frame construction, drainage systems, and decorative arts. The Forbidden City will become the symbolic and administrative center of Ming power.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Archaeological and architectural evidence from the Forbidden City

events.certainty_note: Construction of the Forbidden City proceeded from 1406 to 1420, with 1418 representing a mid-point in the project. The timeline is well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration of African coast accelerates under Henry the Navigator
events.description: Portuguese maritime expeditions, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator (Henrique o Navegador), continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1418, Portuguese ships have established a pattern of coastal reconnaissance and are beginning to establish trading posts and fortifications along the Guinea coast. These voyages represent the beginning of European maritime expansion and the Atlantic slave trade, driven by commercial interest in gold, spices, and enslaved people. The expeditions employ improved navigation technology (astrolabe, caravel design) and establish a pattern of coastal fortification and trade monopoly.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea (Crónica da Descoberta da Guiné), compiled mid-15th century
Portuguese royal records and maritime logs

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast was ongoing in the 1410s and 1420s, though specific events for 1418 are not always precisely dated. The broader campaign is well-attested; individual voyage dates are sometimes approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Constance is sometimes portrayed as a triumph of papal authority and orthodoxy, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the council's decisions did not resolve the underlying religious and social tensions that motivated reform movements. The condemnation of Jan Hus and other reformers actually contributed to the escalation of conflict, particularly in Bohemia. Some scholars argue that the council's failure to address legitimate grievances regarding church corruption and clerical authority weakened the church's position in Central Europe. The Portuguese exploration of the African coast is sometimes presented as a purely commercial and exploratory enterprise, but modern scholarship emphasizes the role of slavery and the exploitation of African peoples from the beginning of Portuguese contact.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1418 is moderate for Europe and Ming China, but heavily skewed toward elite political, religious, and military events. The record is dominated by court chronicles, administrative records, and later compilations. African, American, and Asian sources outside the Ming sphere and Portuguese sphere are underrepresented. The perspective is heavily skewed toward official court narratives and elite concerns. Oral traditions, non-elite perspectives, and the experiences of common people are largely absent from surviving records. The Council of Constance is well-documented from the perspective of the church hierarchy, but the perspectives of reformers and common people are less well-represented.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Constance's condemnation of religious reformers in 1418 contributed to the outbreak of the Hussite Wars in 1419.
The return of the fourth voyage in 1418 led to the preparation and departure of the fifth voyage.
The completion of the Forbidden City in 1418–1420 enabled the relocation of the court to Beijing in 1420.
Portuguese maritime exploration and the conquest of Ceuta were part of the same broader campaign of Portuguese expansion.
